citation.authors: Di Gregorio, M., Romano, M., Sebillo, M., Vitiello, G., & Vozella, A.
citation.title: Improving Human Ground Control Performance in Unmanned Aerial Systems

extraction_metadata.notes: First article this session with a genuinely Proximate human-human communication channel (two co-located operators at a shared MultiTouch wall), distinct from the operator-to-drone channel, which remains Remote. Re-coded 2026-07-10 against Task-Environment v0.8: this article is the codebook's own cited source example for the new Monitoring/Maintenance Task (4.6) code; per that code's scope note, Section 3 is now coded Not Applicable for the battery event rather than forced into Static/Fixed Task Object, since it concerns the system's own internal status, not an external task-object.

study_type.justification: A formal 2x2 between-subjects experiment with 40 participants (SAGAT situation-awareness scores, drones-returned-home performance, team-communication frequency, all statistically analyzed) is the article's central contribution, so User Study is Primary; the MultiTouch-wall GCS interface and its three-tier architecture are substantially described and framed as reusable fleet-management infrastructure building on the authors' prior work, so System/Architecture Paper is Secondary.

summary.task: Two-person operator teams jointly monitored a simulated persistent-surveillance mission conducted by a fleet of five drones detecting an area and reporting to a Ground Control Station, with one team member directing/monitoring drone positions and targets on a shared map and the other monitoring each drone's battery status, across two 4-minute trials (one routine, one with a simulated unexpected low-battery event).
summary.control: Teams used a MultiTouch-wall GCS interface (a three-tier NodeJS web app) showing drone positions/targets on a map plus per-drone status cards (ID, next target, coordinates, battery, a manual recall-to-takeoff button, an artificial-horizon view). At 30% battery a notification alerts the team; at 10% the affected drone automatically returns to and lands at takeoff without further input. The display was manipulated between 'Shared' (both team members see the same full screen) and 'Non-shared' (each sees only their own task-relevant information) conditions.
summary.participants: 40 University of Salerno students (25 male, 15 female, mean age 23.7), all amateur-level UAS pilots and volunteers, randomly assigned within a 2x2 between-subjects design (Shared/Non-shared display x No-event/Event) forming two-person teams, with within-team control-role assignment also randomized and undisclosed to participants.
summary.results: Shared displays produced no difference in performance or situation awareness during routine (No-event) trials, but significantly helped in off-nominal (Event) trials: non-shared teams had significantly worse SAGAT projection scores (Q3, p = .003), returned significantly fewer drones home (Mann-Whitney p = .045), and asked significantly more clarifying questions of each other (t-test p = .002) than shared-display teams specifically when the unexpected low-battery event occurred, with no such differences in the routine condition.
summary.issues: The shared-display advantage appeared only in off-nominal conditions, so its benefit may not be obvious from routine-operation performance alone, potentially undermining adoption arguments. Shared MultiTouch-wall displays require a larger, co-located physical space for the operator team, a practical deployment constraint the authors note explicitly. Only two-operator teams were studied, though real-world control rooms may involve more collaborators. The fleet was homogeneous (five identical drones) over a short (4-minute), relatively simple scenario, leaving open how results generalize to larger, heterogeneous, or longer missions.
summary.recommendations: Prioritize shared-display investment specifically for off-nominal/emergency resilience rather than expecting routine-operation gains. Investigate scenarios with more than two collaborating operators. Extend testing to wider monitored areas, heterogeneous drones with differing performance/payload, and operators distributed across different physical locations rather than only the co-located two-person case tested here. Further investigate the mechanism behind the shared-display advantage (e.g., faster event detection vs. better post-detection problem-solving).
summary.autonomous_capabilities: Each drone autonomously proceeds toward its algorithm-assigned next target and automatically returns to and lands at the take-off point without operator intervention once its battery reaches the critical 10% threshold (with only an informational notification at 30%); the operator otherwise retains decision authority over when to recall a drone early, sharing responsibility for drone status/landing decisions with the system under an established liability-allocation scheme, except at the minimum battery threshold where the system takes automatic control.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The mission is described as 'detection of an area' with drones proceeding to a sequence of targets, but the article does not describe whether these targets are pre-known fixed points or unknown entities discovered during the mission, or how they are spatially/temporally structured.

The manipulated 'unexpected event' — a single drone's battery suddenly dropping — concerns the system's own internal status, not an external environmental task-object, so this dimension does not conceptually apply; per Section 4.6's scope note (added in Task-Environment codebook v0.8), Monitoring/Maintenance Task behavior is coded Not Applicable here rather than forced into Static/Fixed Task Object.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: The operator's required response to a drone's battery event is detecting and responding to the system's own internal health/status change — matching Section 4.6 (Monitoring/Maintenance Task), added in v0.8, for which this article is the codebook's own cited source example.

team_size.justification: Two-person operator teams jointly supervise a fleet of five drones.

team_composition.justification: The fleet consists of five identical drones; heterogeneous drones with differing performance/payload are explicitly named as future work, implying the tested fleet is not heterogeneous.

The two team members have distinct standing roles — one directs drones/targets, the other monitors battery status — differing in responsibility even though both are human operators of similar background.

interaction_model.justification: Each individual team member's role (directing drones, or monitoring battery) addresses/monitors the whole five-drone fleet at once.
The two human operators and the five-drone fleet are all connected through the same shared (or, in the other condition, partially shared) GCS system simultaneously.

interaction_directness.justification: Both the operator-to-drone commands (e.g., recall button) and the operator-to-operator verbal questions are explicitly addressed to their recipient.

communication_proximity.justification: The two team members are physically co-located at the same MultiTouch wall and communicate verbally with each other during the task.
Operator-to-drone interaction is entirely through the simulated screen-based interface, with no physical drones present.

coordination_architecture.justification: The article does not describe any mechanism by which the five drones coordinate with one another (e.g., a leader, negotiation, or shared rule); each appears to follow its own preset route independently, but this is not explicitly confirmed.

computational_characteristics.justification: The automatic return-to-takeoff behavior at 10% battery is a fixed if-then rule (battery threshold breached -> autonomous RTH and landing) rather than a computed optimization or learned model.
The mechanism by which 'the algorithm' sequences a drone's next target is mentioned only in passing, with no algorithm family specified.

human_involvement_level.justification: Drones move toward algorithm-assigned targets without requiring continuous operator input; the operator monitors and can recall a drone early but the function does not depend on this.
Above the critical battery threshold, the operator is explicitly described as being in charge of deciding when a drone should land or return home.

At the 10% critical threshold the system automatically returns and lands the drone without waiting for operator input, but still interfaces with the human via a notification, rather than operating with zero human interface at any stage.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Both directing a drone toward a target and recalling a drone home are discrete, one-off goal-level inputs (e.g., clicking a recall button), with the system executing all subsequent navigation steps; no continuous control channel is described in this study.

operational_domain.justification: All vehicles in the fleet are aerial drones.

flags.note: No description of whether/how the five drones coordinate with one another beyond each following its own preset route.
